steps.title: Spot the Causal Relationship Between Opinion and Behavior
steps.content:
The passage opens with favorable opinions—Mr. Ely is regarded as having remarkable powers, learning, and discretion.

It then pivots to his cautious behaviors: he avoids warm discussion, suggests ideas rather than stating them, hides his laughter, and never creates grounds for ridicule.

The decisive signal is that these behaviors directly enable him to preserve the favorable impression.

steps.title: Apply This Relationship to the Choices
steps.content:
D identifies the structure precisely: the passage presents the favorable opinions others hold, then describes Mr.

Ely's specific behaviors that enable him to maintain that impression.

  • A falsely invokes a decline in regard that never occurs
  • B confuses the Milby/London hint with an explanation of naiveté
  • C notes the public/private gap but wrongly claims the passage explains his motivation
.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
